broad guidance: Identify hand placement and bracing. Look at where a person would place their hands. If the hands overlap at a single point (the pistol grip), it is likely a handgun. If the hands are spread apart in two different locations, look for a buttstock at the rear designed to be braced against the shoulder. Also, check if the weapon is supported by a mechanical stand, such as a two-legged bipod or a three-legged tripod

Infer internal design from external proxies. Since the inside of the barrel is rarely visible, look at the weapon's overall purpose and its ammunition. Rifled weapons typically have thicker-walled barrels and fire pointed or rounded metal bullets. Smoothbore weapons (primarily shotguns) often have thinner-walled barrels with a wider diameter and fire plastic "shells" or shotshells that contain many small pellets.

Look for "self-cycling" vs. "user-cycled" parts. To determine if it cycles automatically, look for a charging handle (a small tab for moving internal parts) or a pistol’s slide (the entire top half of the gun that moves backward). If it must be cycled manually, look for prominent external handles—such as a bolt handle sticking out the side, a sliding handgrip under the barrel, or a lever under the trigger—that a shooter must move after every shot.
Identify the specific part used to operate the gun. Locate the primary mechanical "action" visible in the image
